---
title: "Why importance splats onto the last node"
subtitle: "A Markov explanation in one chain—and where the argument stops"
toc: true
toc-depth: 2
---

::: {.page-lead}
**Short answer.** In a DAG, the variables nearest the outcome can screen off
their ancestors for prediction. A predictor therefore loses nothing by ignoring
the ancestors once it sees the relevant mediator or Markov boundary. SHAP then
faithfully explains that predictor. An intervention answers a different
question, because changing an ancestor also changes its descendants.
:::

## The three-node proof

Take the linear structural causal model

$$
X=\varepsilon_X, \qquad
M=aX+\varepsilon_M, \qquad
Y=bM+\varepsilon_Y,
$$

with mutually independent, mean-zero errors. Its graph is
$X\rightarrow M\rightarrow Y$.

### 1. The mediator screens off the cause for prediction

By d-separation and the causal Markov condition,

$$Y\perp X\mid M.$$

Therefore the Bayes predictor with both $X$ and $M$ available is

$$
f(x,m)=\mathbb E[Y\mid X=x,M=m]
=\mathbb E[Y\mid M=m]
=bm.
$$

Once $M$ is known, $X$ supplies no additional predictive information. This is
the same principle that makes a Markov boundary an optimal feature set: it makes
the target conditionally invariant to features outside the boundary. See
[Margaritis (NeurIPS 2009)](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2009/file/6da37dd3139aa4d9aa55b8d237ec5d4a-Paper.pdf)
for a formal feature-selection treatment.

### 2. Ordinary model attribution sees a dummy feature

For marginal or standard “interventional” model SHAP, the coalition value
evaluates the fitted function while averaging dropped inputs from a background
distribution:

$$
v_{\mathrm{model}}(S)
=\mathbb E\left[f(x_S,X_{\bar S})\right].
$$

Because $f(x,m)=bm$ does not use $x$, adding $X$ changes no coalition value.
By the Shapley dummy axiom,

$$\phi_X^{\mathrm{model}}=0.$$

All model credit is available to land on $M$, the last observed node used by the
predictor. This is what we call the **last-node splat**.

### 3. The upstream intervention still works

Under $do(X=x)$, the structural equation for $M$ remains active:

$$
\mathbb E[Y\mid do(X=x)]
=b\,\mathbb E[M\mid do(X=x)]
=abx.
$$

So the total causal derivative is

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\mathbb E[Y\mid do(X=x)] = ab,
$$

which is nonzero whenever both arrows carry signal. **Conditioning on the
mediator blocks the predictive path; intervening on the ancestor activates the
same path.** There is no contradiction because the two calculations use
different operators and answer different questions.

::: {.pullquote}
The splat is a predictable consequence of a successful predictor: Markov
screening compresses prediction toward the outcome, while intervention effects
continue to propagate from upstream.
:::

## What changes across SHAP variants

| Value function | What can happen to upstream $X$ | What it explains |
|---|---|---|
| **Marginal / model-interventional** $\mathbb E[f(x_S,X_{\bar S})]$ | Exactly zero in the proof above, because $f$ ignores $X$. | Use of inputs by the fitted model. |
| **Conditional** $\mathbb E[f(X)\mid X_S=x_S]$ | May share credit with $X$ through the observed correlation $X\leftrightarrow M$. | Predictive information under the observational distribution; not total causal effect. |
| **Asymmetric / ordered** | Can move credit toward ancestors by restricting feature permutations. | An attribution that respects a chosen ordering. |
| **Structural / causal** $\mathbb E[f(X)\mid do(X_S=x_S)]$ | Captures the change in unfixed descendants after $do(X=x)$. | Direct plus propagated indirect contribution under an SCM. |

The distinction between observational and interventional distributions for
dropped features is developed by
[Janzing, Minorics & Blöbaum (AISTATS 2020)](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v108/janzing20a.html).
[Frye, Rowat & Feige (NeurIPS 2020)](https://papers.nips.cc/paper_files/paper/2020/hash/0d770c496aa3da6d2c3f2bd19e7b9d6b-Abstract.html)
introduce asymmetric Shapley values that encode a causal ordering.

Most directly, [Heskes et al. (NeurIPS 2020)](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper/2020/file/32e54441e6382a7fbacbbbaf3c450059-Paper.pdf)
define

$$
v_{do}(S)=\mathbb E[f(X)\mid do(X_S=x_S)]
$$

and decompose causal Shapley values into direct and indirect effects. Their own
chain example uses a predictor that ignores the upstream feature and shows why
marginal SHAP cannot represent its indirect contribution. Our repository does
not claim to invent that principle; it asks whether implementing it improves
**intervention-target recovery** on known DAG simulations.

## Why a downstream proxy can look even worse

Now add $Z=cY+\varepsilon_Z$, so $Z$ is a descendant of the outcome. Intervening
on $Z$ cannot change its ancestor $Y$ in an acyclic SCM:

$$
\mathbb E[Y\mid do(Z=z)]-\mathbb E[Y]=0.
$$

Yet $Z$ can be extremely predictive of $Y$. A fitted model may use it when the
true mediator is noisy, partially measured, regularized, or represented through
interactions. SHAP can then give $Z$ substantial credit while its total effect on
$Y$ is exactly zero. `ClinicVisit` plays this deliberately dramatic role in the
[teaching demonstration](index.qmd#the-demonstration).

This is not a failure of the Shapley axioms. It is an **estimand mismatch**:
model contribution is being read as intervention leverage.

## When the splat should weaken

The exact zero in the proof relies on a clean chain, observed $M$, correct
conditioning, and a well-estimated predictor. Upstream variables may retain
predictive SHAP mass when:

- $X$ also has a direct path to $Y$;
- mediators are missing or measured with error;
- the fitted model is finite, regularized, or misspecified;
- latent variables create dependencies not represented by the DAG;
- the explanation uses conditional rather than marginal background semantics.

Those cases change the degree of the splat, not the central distinction. No
observational SHAP variant becomes a total-effect estimator merely because it
assigns some credit upstream.

## From causal attribution to action

Even structural attribution is not yet a recommendation. A recommendation must
specify an action set, cost, feasibility constraints, desired outcome, and
uncertainty. [Karimi, Schölkopf & Valera (FAccT 2021)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2002.06278)
make this shift explicit: nearest counterfactual states tell a person where a
favorable case is, while causal interventions describe how to get there.

Their follow-on work on
[recourse under imperfect causal knowledge (NeurIPS 2020)](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper/2020/file/02a3c7fb3f489288ae6942498498db20-Paper.pdf)
also proves a vital limit: without the true structural equations, recourse
cannot generally be guaranteed when intervened variables have descendants.
That is why this project calls its output a target-recovery demonstration—not a
deployed recommendation engine.

::: {.next-step}
**Next:** [See what the current experiments establish](evidence.qmd), including
the null result that causal ordering alone does not solve target recovery.
:::
